title: Hardwood
alt: They may technically have been softwoods at some point, but they definitely aren't now.
transcript:
[Cueball is looking down and pointing with a finger towards the floor in front of Blondie who is facing him. Behind her is a picture on the wall showing seven sedimentary layers above each other, assuming the top of the picture is air. Each layer has a different texture than the ones it borders but two are as white as the air at the top, including the bottom-most layer.]
Cueball: So you're saying that if we tear up these layers, there's hardwood flooring under here?
Blondie: ...I mean, sort of...

[Caption below the panel:]
Home remodelers learn about the Carboniferous
explanation:
Home remodelers, whether contractors or do-it-yourselfers, sometimes seek to remove layers of modifications to restore some previous condition. Flooring made of hardwood (oak, maple, etc.) panels, perhaps deprecated by earlier homeowners for comfort or style reasons, can be prized by later homeowners as tastes or fashions change. Hardwood floors are currently considered desirable in homes for durability, aesthetic appeal and other reasons, and may have been the original choice for the building, but aspirations constantly change and intermediate decoration may have overlaid carpetting or / at a time that this was considered more fashionable or practical.

In this comic Blondie has apparently explained  to Cueball, who refers to finding a “hardwood floor” under these layers. The joke is that the characters are talking about the , rather than about a house.  The “hardwood floor” is actually a   from the  period, 300-360 million years ago, that originated as woody plants and other material. The “layers” are overlying geological strata that are likely hundreds or thousands of meters thick, making it impractical, if not impossible, to tear them up, and making it unlikely that there is any aesthetic reason to expose the “hardwood” layer.

The title text refers to the fact that typical  would all be classified as  or even not woody at all; flowering plants (), the principal source of today's , would not appear on Earth in any significant numbers for another 220 million years. However, whatever it would be classified as in the beginning, it is now “hardwood” in the literal sense of wood that is hard, as it is now all coal and other rock.

The idea of using geology in relation to houses is also used the Home Inspections series and in 3070: Orogeny, all released relatively close to each other.
